content:
**Richard Bellman** coined **"curse of dimensionality"** around **1961** in **Adaptive Control Processes**, describing how **dynamic programming** on a **grid** over a **d**-dimensional **state space** requires **exponentially** many cells as **d** grows — the **value function** cannot be stored or updated densely.

Statisticians and **pattern recognition** researchers encountered the same **combinatorial** barrier in **multivariate** **density estimation** and **nonparametric** smoothing: **kernel** and **k-NN** methods need **local** samples, but **local** regions are **empty** in high **d** unless **n** is enormous.

**Huber** and others formalized **nonparametric** **regression** in increasing dimensions (1970s–80s), clarifying **minimax** rates that worsen with **d**.

**Aggarwal**, **Hinneburg**, and **Keim** (early 2000s) documented **distance concentration** and **hubness** in **high-dimensional** **vector** data, explaining empirical failures of **similarity search**.

**Machine learning** practice (1990s–present) absorbed the lesson into **PCA**, **feature selection**, **regularization**, and **manifold** methods — the phrase moved from **operations research** grids to everyday **ML** vocabulary as **feature engineering** exploded.

Today the term anchors **why** **deep embeddings** and **tabular** **pipelines** still need **disciplined dimension** even when **compute** is cheap — **geometry** does not waive **sample complexity**.

timeline.label: Bellman names the curse (1961)
timeline.description: Adaptive Control Processes introduces 'curse of dimensionality' for exponential grid growth in multi-dimensional dynamic programming.

timeline.label: Nonparametric statistics (1970s–80s)
timeline.description: Kernel and nearest-neighbor theory shows local estimators need sample sizes that explode with dimension for fixed accuracy.

timeline.label: Pattern recognition & ML textbooks (1990s)
timeline.description: ESL, Bishop, and related texts popularize the curse for k-NN, clustering, and density estimation in ML courses.

timeline.label: Distance concentration research (2001+)
timeline.description: Empirical and theoretical work on hubness and vanishing distance contrast in high-dimensional similarity search.

timeline.label: Modern high-d pipelines (2010s–present)
timeline.description: PCA, embeddings, and regularized deep models standardize mitigation as tabular and representation learning routinely face n ≪ d.
